activity_metadata.objective: Students will determine the central idea of paragraph 17 from "Hatshepsut: His Majesty, Herself" and analyze how supporting details convey that central idea.
activity_metadata.alignment_long: This activity replaces the "Your Turn" segment from the StudySync Central or Main Idea Skills lesson for "Hatshepsut: His Majesty, Herself." Students analyze how supporting details in paragraph 17 convey the central idea about Hatshepsut's approach to ruling Egypt.
activity_metadata.connection_to_end_of_unit_assessment_tas: This activity directly supports the end-of-unit assessment by developing students' ability to determine central idea and analyze how it is conveyed through particular details. The end-of-unit assessment includes multiple passages that test Central or Main Idea alongside other skills, requiring students to identify main points and distinguish them from supporting details. The Part A/Part B question format in this activity mirrors the assessment structure, giving students practice with both identifying central ideas AND selecting textual evidence that supports those ideas - critical skills for success on the unit assessment.
activity_metadata.unit_level_connection: This activity connects to the unit's essential question "How do we know what to do when there are no instructions?" by examining how Hatshepsut made unprecedented decisions about leadership without a rulebook to follow. By analyzing the central idea that Hatshepsut promoted peace through monument-building rather than military conquest, students explore how a leader created her own approach when traditional pharaoh strategies didn't align with her values. Understanding central idea helps students recognize how authors convey the significance of people who forge new paths - a key theme throughout this unit's exploration of individuals who navigate uncertain territory without clear instructions.
activity_metadata.excerpt:
Excerpt from “Hatshepsut: His Majesty, Herself”

Paragraph 17

(17) With the exception of one military campaign against Nubia, Hatshepsut’s reign was peaceful. Instead of expanding Egypt’s borders through war and conquest, Hatshepsut built monuments within her country to proclaim its power. Her masterpiece was the magnificent temple at the site known today as Deir el-Bahri. The temple was dedicated to Amen, the god who was supposed to be the divine father of every pharaoh, the god to whom Hatshepsut felt she owed her good fortune. The temple at Deir el-Bahri was said to be Hatshepsut’s own mortuary temple. The building is set into the side of a mountain and rises gracefully in three beautifully proportioned tiers, each supported by columns like those to be seen centuries later in Greek temples. Its design was far ahead of its time. Hatshepsut called it Djeser-Djeseru (JEH-sir jeh-SEH-roo)—“Holy of Holies.”
exit_ticket: Which of the following statements best portrays the central idea of the paragraph?
Which line from the paragraph best supports your answer to the previous question?

questions_map.question: What does the author tell us in paragraph 17 about Hatshepsut's approach to ruling Egypt?
The author creates a contrast in the opening sentences between what Hatshepsut didn't do and what she did do. What does this contrast reveal about her values as a leader?
The author states that Hatshepsut built monuments, “to proclaim [Egypt's] power.” Based on the details about the temple at Deir el-Bahri, how could a monument proclaim power?
How do the details about the temple at Deir el-Bahri connect to Hatshepsut's approach to ruling?
Which of the following statements best portrays the central idea of the paragraph?
Which line from the paragraph best supports your answer to the previous question?
